title: Justice Will Rise Sky-High; the Drum of Victory Will Resound!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil section below is a corrected reading copy; the scanned page remains authoritative and the canonical Tamil chapter remains unchanged. The stray Latin fragment `STUNG` was visually verified against the source as `தீர்ப்பிலே` (“in the judgment”) and has been restored here. The malformed percentage line remains limited to what the source evidence supports. Page-break fragments are joined in English without changing paragraph order or substance.
paragraphs: Jayalalithaa has once again assumed office as Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu amid celebrations by party members brought into Chennai and gathered there, without the slightest concern for the inconvenience caused to the public or for legal rules and procedures. Was she now elected Chief Minister after a general election to the Legislative Assembly, democratically securing a majority of the people's votes? No. For nineteen years, a case had proceeded against Jayalalithaa for amassing assets worth Rs. 66 crore beyond her known income. Finally, on 27 September last year, Special Court Judge Cunha in Bengaluru sentenced her to four years' imprisonment and imposed a fine of Rs. 100 crore, following which she was imprisoned. Jayalalithaa sought release on bail, and the matter went up to the Supreme Court. Without even hearing the Karnataka Government, whose view ought properly to have been sought, Chief Justice Dattu heard the matter with extraordinary speed, accepted requests that had not been placed before the Court, and released Jayalalithaa on bail on 17 October 2014.

When the Chief Justice thus released Jayalalithaa on bail, he also set a remarkable precedent not followed in similar cases: he directed the Karnataka High Court to hear and decide Jayalalithaa's appeal alone within three months. Even then, severe criticism of both that order and Chief Justice Dattu appeared in newspapers throughout India.

Thereafter, Jayalalithaa's appeal was heard by a Special Bench of the Karnataka High Court from 5 January to 11 March. During the hearings, Justice Kumaraswamy repeatedly made observations to the lawyers appearing for the accused that reflected the direction of his thinking, and those observations were continuously reported in the press. In particular, he openly said that their arguments had produced no evidence capable of completely refuting the charges; that they had not untied any of the more than 150 knots in Judge Cunha's judgment; that their arguments had not earned sufficient marks; and that he appeared to have no option but to repeat Cunha's judgment. From the adverse observations he voluntarily made on different days, people from many quarters believed they could foresee what the Karnataka High Court's verdict would be.

Meanwhile, a Constitution Bench of the Supreme Court, comprising Justices Dipak Misra, R. K. Agrawal and Prafulla C. Pant, delivered a judgment that referred to the forthcoming Karnataka High Court verdict. It offered forceful guidance: “The High Court Judge [Kumaraswamy] must bear in mind the gravity of the offence alleged against the accused. The High Court must remain conscious of how corruption is eating into society like termites. It must evaluate all the evidence before it fully and collectively before delivering judgment. A judgment rendered without fear or favour must set out its conclusions firmly and without bias.”

Indeed, in the case that led to this Constitution Bench, Supreme Court Justice Madan B. Lokur had pointed to the damage this proceeding was causing to the judicial system: “This appeal is an example of how a criminal case should not be conducted. The system for delivering justice in criminal cases has failed. It must be repaired. The time for that has arrived.”
Why did the Supreme Court Bench make observations so extraordinary that they had scarcely been heard in any other case?

A few days before the High Court judgment, reports circulated widely that the Chief Justice of India himself had visited Bengaluru and that various efforts were under way to secure a judgment favourable to the accused Jayalalithaa. We did not believe those reports in the slightest. On 11 May 2015, Justice Kumaraswamy of the Karnataka High Court acquitted Jayalalithaa in his judgment. AIADMK members celebrated it as a victory, believing the unexpected had happened. Jayalalithaa placed a double lock upon her conscience, sent it into deep sleep, and issued a statement declaring that justice had triumphed. For our part, we wondered at the gulf, as vast as that between a mountain and a hollow, separating Justice Kumaraswamy's words during the hearing from the judgment he delivered. But the very next day, in developments startling enough to overturn the judgment itself, many people fearlessly and openly identified its grave errors and confusion. When I studied the judgment closely, I too found an enormous mathematical error and realised that this error formed the very foundation of the verdict.

When the Pandya king Nedunchezhiyan realised that he had delivered a wrongful judgment, he confessed from his heart, “Am I a king? I am the thief!”, fell from his throne and gave up his life. Thus the celebrated name “Nedunchezhiyan, who never deviated from justice” became permanent. He knew justice; he felt justice!

Justice Kumaraswamy listed ten loans that Jayalalithaa's side had obtained from Indian Bank and added them up as Rs. 24 crore. Treating that sum as income, he wrote that Jayalalithaa's disproportionate assets amounted to only eight per cent of her actual income and that, when assets beyond income were below ten per cent, there was no need to regard the matter as an offence and impose punishment. On that basis, he acquitted Jayalalithaa and the other accused. If these details had been true and capable of proof, there could have been no two opinions about her acquittal. But the figure of Rs. 24 crore stated in Justice Kumaraswamy's judgment as the total of the Indian Bank loans is completely wrong. Add those ten loans, and the sum is only Rs. 10 crore. There is no possibility whatever of obtaining the Rs. 24 crore total stated by the Judge. On the true total, Jayalalithaa had amassed assets 76 per cent above her income. It has also been argued that even the figure of 76 per cent is wrong and that, once every error is included, her disproportionate assets exceeded 119 per cent, for which she ought to have been punished.

That is not all. Many people explained in detail that the Prevention of Corruption Act currently in force contains no rule exempting disproportionate assets merely because they fall within ten per cent, and that no such provision appeared in the amendment enacted in 1988 when Rajiv Gandhi was Prime Minister. Pointing to all these facts, they insist that the very basis of the verdict contains a Himalayan blunder, that the judgment must therefore be reversed, and that the Karnataka Government, which has played the principal first-line role in the case, must appeal to the Supreme Court.

On 22 May 2015, senior journalist A. Subramaniam wrote a detailed article in *The Times of India* about Justice Kumaraswamy's judgment under the headings “Curious Case of Amma Assets: Overlooking the Obvious” and “From Calculation to Take on Gifts, Karnataka High Court Order Has Many Loopholes.” Journalist Sandhya Ravishankar, after studying the case closely, published a long article titled “More than Bad Maths: Four Big Errors That Let Jayalalithaa off the Hook.” Several newspapers have stated that the Karnataka High Court's 919-page judgment contains many unusual assertions and conclusions. I shall briefly set out only a few of their unanswerable arguments.

Justice Kumaraswamy's judgment states that the ten Indian Bank loans taken by Jayalalithaa's side totalled Rs. 24 crore. But whoever adds those ten loans obtains only Rs. 10 crore. By inflating Rs. 10 crore in actual loans to Rs. 24 crore, he acquitted Jayalalithaa and the others. The judgment therefore added an excess Rs. 14 crore to the total.

Of those ten loans, the Rs. 1.5 crore loan obtained from Indian Bank for Jaya Publications had been repaid, and the trial court had already accepted that repayment as a legitimate expenditure. At serial number eight, Justice Kumaraswamy's judgment showed Sudhakaran as having received a loan of Rs. 1.57 crore. The truth, however, is that although Sudhakaran applied to Indian Bank for Rs. 1.57 crore, evidence shows that the Bank sanctioned only Rs. 1.33 crore. When the Special Court tried the case, it counted only the Rs. 1.33 crore actually received. Justice Kumaraswamy instead treated the Rs. 1.57 crore requested in the application as though it had been received, used that figure in his calculations, and acquitted the accused.

Justice Kumaraswamy accepted the filing of income-tax returns and the payment of income tax as proof of income. But in the 2014 Suresh Rajan case, the Supreme Court held that payment of income tax alone could not prove that assets had been acquired through honest means. *The Times of India* therefore said that, in light of the Supreme Court's ruling, Justice Kumaraswamy was wrong to accept income-tax returns and tax payments as proof of income in this case.

A retired judge observed: “In several disproportionate-assets cases, the Supreme Court has made clear that the source from which income arose must itself be established in an acceptable manner. The Income Tax Department does not investigate the source of income. An income-tax return therefore cannot be treated as documentary proof of the source.”

Justice Kumaraswamy accepted as Jayalalithaa's income gifts worth Rs. 1.5 crore received for her forty-fourth birthday. *The Times of India* wrote that this was contrary to ethical rules. Paying income tax cannot cleanse illegal assets of their stain. Conduct rules governing public servants prohibit the acceptance of gifts. If gifts are recognised as a lawful source of income, the central purpose of the Prevention of Corruption Act itself will be defeated, the English daily observed.

It was astonishing not only that Justice Kumaraswamy accepted belated income-tax returns, but also that he counted birthday gifts and money sent from abroad as income. Foreign remittances of Rs. 77 lakh had been investigated by the CBI, and a case concerning them had remained pending before the Supreme Court since 2012. Yet Justice Kumaraswamy independently treated the money as income before that case was decided and acquitted Jayalalithaa. Under the Foreign Exchange Regulation Act (FERA), a person serving as a minister is completely prohibited from receiving gifts from foreign countries. Jayalalithaa's receipt of Rs. 77 lakh in gifts while serving as Chief Minister was therefore an offence under FERA. Justice Kumaraswamy delivered his judgment without regard for the pending Supreme Court case or respect for FERA.

It has also been said that buildings constructed at a cost of Rs. 28 crore were valued by Justice Kumaraswamy at the drastically reduced figure of Rs. 5 crore. *The Times of India* explained that no receipts, vouchers, engineers' reports or declarations from the builders were filed to prove such a reduction. It was wrong for Justice Kumaraswamy to reject government-notified prices for construction materials. Since courts ordinarily accept the rates fixed by the Government's Public Works Department, his refusal to accept the PWD valuation creates the suspicion that he strained to favour the accused, disregarding important judgments and laws.

On page 875 of his own judgment, Justice Kumaraswamy wrote that “an income-tax return filed as an afterthought is not trustworthy” and that failure to file returns for many years deprived the taxpayer of the relevant claim. He added that filing after a long delay, instead of at the proper time, created grave doubt about the genuineness of the *Namadhu MGR* subscription scheme. Yet he accepted Rs. 4 crore as income under that very scheme. Is that not self-contradictory? Does it not make one wonder whether the *Namadhu MGR* subscription scheme was devised on the assumption that a person could wait until charges were framed, then file an income-tax return combining every unidentifiable receipt and thereby escape the grip of the law? The retired judge also said that the judiciary must remain alert to the effect of such conduct upon our social, economic and political structures.

NDTV reported that the valuation of Jayalalithaa's disproportionate assets had been reduced by one-third merely by altering the cost assigned to laying the foundations of the buildings. By reducing the rate from Rs. 310 to Rs. 250 per square foot, the valuation was lowered by Rs. 22 crore, it revealed.

Senior Supreme Court advocate Karunanandhi declared categorically: “The Karnataka High Court misunderstood the Supreme Court's judgment in the Krishnanand Agnihotri case. Justice Kumaraswamy suggested that an accused is entitled to acquittal if disproportionate assets are within ten per cent of income. Nowhere does the Supreme Court order say so.”

Meanwhile, it is said that efforts are under way to silence the law itself by preventing this case from proceeding to the Supreme Court in appeal, the natural next stage prescribed by law. Even if there had been no grave error in the judgment’s total, which anyone could easily understand, one might have argued that no appeal was necessary. But when an enormous error has occurred and everyone can see it, is it lawful for Jayalalithaa hurriedly to resume office as Chief Minister on the basis of that erroneous judgment, without an appeal? Rather than walking upright along the straight road to assume office, is this grand celebration for tying the law's legs, bidding farewell to conscience, and entering office through the back door? Did the Governor of Tamil Nadu, the representative of the Constitution, cooperate in Jayalalithaa's assumption of office on the basis of a wrongful judgment? Because the BJP, which governs at the Centre, attended the swearing-in, does that mean the Union Government too endorses this wrong?

Why did Jayalalithaa not take office immediately as first planned? Because she herself knew perfectly well that the judgment was wrong. Yet, in keeping with the saying that the desire for office can make anyone dance, she later assumed office. And even that was filled with violations!

This was not the first time Jayalalithaa had assumed office contrary to the Constitution. Before the 2001 general election, a Special Court sentenced her on 9 October 2000 to three years' imprisonment in the TANSI land-deal corruption case. Under the rule disqualifying anyone sentenced to more than two years' imprisonment from contesting an election, legal experts said that Jayalalithaa could not contest. She filed a petition in the Madras High Court on 3 November 2000, seeking a stay of the Special Court judgment and permission to contest the election.

Justice Malai Subramanian dismissed her petition on 11 April 2001. He observed that while he could stay a conviction under Section 409 of the Indian Penal Code, he could not stay the Special Court's judgment under the Prevention of Corruption Act.

Even as the Election Commission of India was considering the matter, Jayalalithaa filed nominations in four constituencies. Because she had filed in four constituencies, the returning officers rejected her nominations as invalid. Jayalalithaa made this the central theme of her campaign across Tamil Nadu, stirring the instinctive sympathy of the Tamil people, and the AIADMK consequently won the election. On 14 May, while results were still coming in, a meeting of AIADMK legislators was convened in great haste and elected her leader of the legislature party. That very evening, Governor Fathima Beevi administered the oath of office to her. Newspapers wrote that the swearing-in was rushed through for fear that someone might approach the court seeking to restrain it. *Ananda Vikatan*, in particular, wrote in an editorial: “Jayalalithaa should occupy the Chief Minister's chair only after legally wiping away the stain upon her. That alone would befit both that dignified and honourable office and Jayalalithaa herself.”

In an editorial dated 15 May 2001, *The Hindu* wrote that this was the first time a person definitively barred from contesting an Assembly election had become Chief Minister, and the first time a person legally convicted by a court had occupied such an office. In both respects, it said, her action violated the fundamental spirit of the Constitution and raised painful questions both legally and morally. Jayalalithaa assumed office then by disregarding all of this.

Now too, she has taken the oath by following the same procedure she adopted in 2001, ignoring the lawful path. To understand the scale on which Jayalalithaa's side accumulated land: the list runs to 53 acres on 22 August 1994; 281 acres on 17 November 1994; 507 acres on 22 December 1994; 166 acres on 6 January 1995; and 158 acres on 21 February 1995. On 17 November 1994 alone, purchases were successively recorded as 281 acres, 73.90 acres, 69.78 acres, 60.65 acres, 42.31 acres and 34.815 acres. Altogether they purchased three thousand acres. The allegation that Jayalalithaa amassed assets worth Rs. 66 crore was the figure stated when this case was filed fifteen years ago. The truth is that, if those assets are valued at today's market rates, Jayalalithaa accumulated property worth more than approximately Rs. 6,000 crore!

Many socially concerned journalists and retired judges have written in the press: “The Karnataka High Court judgment may become a precedent for many erroneous judgments in the future. The Supreme Court must recognise that the world is watching India closely at this moment. Justice Kumaraswamy's judgment has greatly diminished the esteem and dignity enjoyed by the Indian judiciary around the world. The Supreme Court must therefore examine this judgment sharply and deeply.”

Everyone has a duty to establish this truth through law. Legal experts, impartial journalists and social activists strongly desire that the stain now lying upon the judiciary, a vital pillar of democracy, be wiped away. Justice Kumaraswamy's judgment is not the final judgment. It is the Supreme Court of India that must dispel the stupor that has overtaken the god of justice, straighten the law that has been bent, and set it again upon the true path.

Just as the Karnataka Government and Dr Subramanian Swamy bear responsibility for appealing in this case, the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam too has a duty to bow to the expectations of the people of Tamil Nadu and appeal. That is why, at the meeting of District Secretaries held on 25 May 2015, I announced: “Since the Supreme Court has twice categorically declared that the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam has the right to participate in this case, I firmly state that the DMK too will appeal to the Supreme Court in this disproportionate-assets case against Jayalalithaa.” However many stumbling blocks are piled upon the royal road of justice, justice will turn them into stepping stones and, in the end, rise sky-high! The drum of victory will resound!
